        <div id="doc" class="markdown-body container-fluid" data-hard-breaks="false" lang="en-US"><h1 class="part" data-startline="1" data-endline="1" id="當AI-帶來思考外包x認知負債，要如何不讓AI-奴役你---柯仁傑-David-Ko敏捷三叔公" data-id="當AI-帶來思考外包x認知負債，要如何不讓AI-奴役你---柯仁傑-David-Ko敏捷三叔公"><a class="anchor hidden-xs" href="https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg#%E7%95%B6AI-%E5%B8%B6%E4%BE%86%E6%80%9D%E8%80%83%E5%A4%96%E5%8C%85x%E8%AA%8D%E7%9F%A5%E8%B2%A0%E5%82%B5%EF%BC%8C%E8%A6%81%E5%A6%82%E4%BD%95%E4%B8%8D%E8%AE%93AI-%E5%A5%B4%E5%BD%B9%E4%BD%A0---%E6%9F%AF%E4%BB%81%E5%82%91-David-Ko%E6%95%8F%E6%8D%B7%E4%B8%89%E5%8F%94%E5%85%AC" title="當AI-帶來思考外包x認知負債，要如何不讓AI-奴役你---柯仁傑-David-Ko敏捷三叔公"><span class="octicon octicon-link ph ph-link-simple-horizontal"></span></a><span data-position="2" data-size="50">當AI 帶來思考外包x認知負債，要如何不讓AI 奴役你 - 柯仁傑 (David Ko/敏捷三叔公)</span></h1><div class="alert alert-warning">
<p><span data-position="11" data-size="18">Loading embed note</span></p>
</div><h2 class="part" data-startline="5" data-endline="5" id="筆記區" data-id="筆記區"><a class="anchor hidden-xs" href="https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg#%E7%AD%86%E8%A8%98%E5%8D%80" title="筆記區"><span class="octicon octicon-link ph ph-link-simple-horizontal"></span></a><span data-position="92" data-size="3">筆記區</span></h2><h2 class="part" data-startline="7" data-endline="7" id="GenAI-帶來的認知問題" data-id="GenAI-帶來的認知問題"><a class="anchor hidden-xs" href="https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg#GenAI-%E5%B8%B6%E4%BE%86%E7%9A%84%E8%AA%8D%E7%9F%A5%E5%95%8F%E9%A1%8C" title="GenAI-帶來的認知問題"><span class="octicon octicon-link ph ph-link-simple-horizontal"></span></a><span data-position="100" data-size="13">GenAI 帶來的認知問題</span></h2><p class="part" data-startline="8" data-endline="8" data-position="114" data-size="0"><img src="https://hackmd.io/_uploads/r1C9QBqMMe.jpg" alt="PXL_20260625_053311465.MP (1)" class="offline-handled error-handled" data-position="114" data-size="75" loading="lazy"></p><blockquote class="raw part" data-startline="9" data-endline="9">
<p data-position="192" data-size="0"><span data-position="192" data-size="5">越來越發散</span></p>
</blockquote><blockquote class="raw part" data-startline="11" data-endline="11">
<p data-position="201" data-size="0"><span data-position="201" data-size="26">產品越大，人的認知會跟產品全貌有落差 -> 認知落差</span></p>
</blockquote><p class="part" data-startline="13" data-endline="13" data-position="229" data-size="0"><span data-position="229" data-size="26">產品程式現狀 <-> 人對於產品程式現狀，中間的落差</span></p><p class="part" data-startline="15" data-endline="15" data-position="257" data-size="0"><span data-position="257" data-size="24">因為 LLM 的增強的關係，開始出現跟不上的內容</span></p><blockquote class="raw part" data-startline="17" data-endline="17">
<p data-position="285" data-size="0"><span data-position="285" data-size="17">讓你的思考外包，變笨是唯一的結果？</span></p>
</blockquote><p class="part" data-startline="19" data-endline="19" data-position="304" data-size="0"><span data-position="304" data-size="9">刻痕、烙印的重要性</span></p><p class="part" data-startline="22" data-endline="23" data-position="316" data-size="0"><span data-position="316" data-size="6">什麼是認知？</span>
<span data-position="323" data-size="18">從接收訊息到做出反應，中間所有的過程</span></p><p class="part" data-startline="25" data-endline="25" data-position="343" data-size="0"><span data-position="343" data-size="21">認知負債：沒搞懂但卻立刻就有成品之間的落差</span></p><p class="part" data-startline="27" data-endline="27" data-position="366" data-size="0"><span data-position="366" data-size="16">有了AI之後，認知過程就會變了調</span></p><blockquote class="raw part" data-startline="28" data-endline="28">
<p data-position="385" data-size="0"><span data-position="385" data-size="41">一杯咖啡，聞不到咖啡香、不知道自己是冰還熱、不知道上次拉肚子了沒、不知道要點冰還熱</span></p>
</blockquote><h2 class="part" data-startline="31" data-endline="31" id="傳統錯誤的解法" data-id="傳統錯誤的解法"><a class="anchor hidden-xs" href="https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg#%E5%82%B3%E7%B5%B1%E9%8C%AF%E8%AA%A4%E7%9A%84%E8%A7%A3%E6%B3%95" title="傳統錯誤的解法"><span class="octicon octicon-link ph ph-link-simple-horizontal"></span></a><span data-position="432" data-size="7">傳統錯誤的解法</span></h2><ul class="part" data-startline="33" data-endline="56" data-position="441" data-size="0">
<li class="raw" data-startline="33" data-endline="51" data-position="443" data-size="0">
<p data-position="441" data-size="0"><span data-position="443" data-size="31">將團隊職責切分得越細，員工需要學習的東西就越少，認知負荷就越低</span></p>
<blockquote class="raw">
<p data-position="482" data-size="0"><span data-position="482" data-size="10">依照「職能」區分團隊</span></p>
</blockquote>
<ul data-position="498" data-size="0">
<li class="raw" data-startline="37" data-endline="37" data-position="500" data-size="0"><span data-position="500" data-size="34">案例：導入 Scrum，但卻按職能拆團隊，原本職責廣泛的團隊被分割了</span></li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="38" data-endline="38" data-position="541" data-size="0"><span data-position="541" data-size="40">需求量大、複雜後，這些小團隊就動不起來了，容易彼此互卡，依賴關係導致交付時間變長</span></li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="39" data-endline="39" data-position="588" data-size="0"><span data-position="588" data-size="29">軟體組件無法整合，整合DevOps,DevSecOps團隊</span></li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="40" data-endline="40" data-position="624" data-size="0"><span data-position="624" data-size="15">沒人驗證整體品質，增加QA團隊</span></li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="41" data-endline="41" data-position="646" data-size="0"><span data-position="646" data-size="13">產品不符需求，增加客戶支援</span></li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="42" data-endline="43" data-position="666" data-size="0"><span data-position="666" data-size="7">產生踢皮球效應</span></li>
</ul>
<blockquote class="raw">
<p data-position="681" data-size="0"><span data-position="681" data-size="10">多專案併發與情境切換</span></p>
</blockquote>
<ul data-position="697" data-size="0">
<li class="raw" data-startline="46" data-endline="46" data-position="699" data-size="0"><span data-position="699" data-size="35">資源利用率的迷思，為了確保每個組件團隊「有事做」，公司同時啟動多個計畫</span></li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="47" data-endline="48" data-position="741" data-size="0"><span data-position="741" data-size="6">導致吼叫效應</span>
<blockquote class="raw">
<p data-position="758" data-size="0"><span data-position="758" data-size="11">叫得夠大聲才爭得到資源</span></p>
</blockquote>
</li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="49" data-endline="49" data-position="776" data-size="0"><span data-position="776" data-size="11">產生許多無效的管理會議</span></li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="50" data-endline="51" data-position="794" data-size="0"><span data-position="794" data-size="17">分工求生，全端工程師變成孤單工程師</span></li>
</ul>
</li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="52" data-endline="56" data-position="815" data-size="0">
<p data-position="813" data-size="0"><span data-position="815" data-size="27">把 LLM/AI 當成中樞，把弄懂全局的工作交給 AI</span></p>
<ul data-position="847" data-size="0">
<li class="raw" data-startline="53" data-endline="53" data-position="849" data-size="0"><span data-position="849" data-size="19">但這其實也是泰勒(Taylor)的老路</span></li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="54" data-endline="54" data-position="875" data-size="0"><span data-position="875" data-size="9">可能會付出四個代價</span></li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="55" data-endline="56" data-position="891" data-size="0"><img src="https://hackmd.io/_uploads/Hy6OmrcMfx.jpg" alt="PXL_20260625_055051865.MP (1)" class="offline-handled error-handled" data-position="891" data-size="75" loading="lazy"></li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul><h2 class="part" data-startline="57" data-endline="57" id="持續學習和回饋才是正解" data-id="持續學習和回饋才是正解"><a class="anchor hidden-xs" href="https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg#%E6%8C%81%E7%BA%8C%E5%AD%B8%E7%BF%92%E5%92%8C%E5%9B%9E%E9%A5%8B%E6%89%8D%E6%98%AF%E6%AD%A3%E8%A7%A3" title="持續學習和回饋才是正解"><span class="octicon octicon-link ph ph-link-simple-horizontal"></span></a><span data-position="971" data-size="11">持續學習和回饋才是正解</span></h2><ul class="part" data-startline="59" data-endline="76" data-position="984" data-size="0">
<li class="raw" data-startline="59" data-endline="61" data-position="986" data-size="0">
<p data-position="984" data-size="0"><span data-position="986" data-size="25">Spaced Repetition：穩定知識的固著</span></p>
<ul data-position="1016" data-size="0">
<li class="raw" data-startline="60" data-endline="61" data-position="1018" data-size="0"><img src="https://hackmd.io/_uploads/BJwoUS9Mzx.jpg" alt="PXL_20260625_055139230.MP (1)" class="offline-handled error-handled" data-position="1018" data-size="75" loading="lazy"></li>
</ul>
</li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="62" data-endline="66" data-position="1097" data-size="0">
<p data-position="1095" data-size="0"><span data-position="1097" data-size="30">多團隊 Product Backlog Refinement</span></p>
<ul data-position="1132" data-size="0">
<li class="raw" data-startline="63" data-endline="63" data-position="1134" data-size="0"><span data-position="1134" data-size="8">LeSS 的概念</span></li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="64" data-endline="64" data-position="1149" data-size="0"><span data-position="1149" data-size="5">從實例解釋</span></li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="65" data-endline="66" data-position="1161" data-size="0"><img src="https://hackmd.io/_uploads/S1tWXB5zze.jpg" alt="IMG_8200" class="offline-handled error-handled" data-position="1161" data-size="54" loading="lazy"></li>
</ul>
</li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="67" data-endline="69" data-position="1219" data-size="0">
<p data-position="1217" data-size="0"><span data-position="1219" data-size="7">傳話遊戲的死穴</span></p>
<ul data-position="1231" data-size="0">
<li class="raw" data-startline="68" data-endline="69" data-position="1233" data-size="0"><span data-position="1233" data-size="21">新角色：Product Developer</span>
<ul data-position="1263" data-size="0">
<li class="raw" data-startline="69" data-endline="69" data-position="1265" data-size="0"><span data-position="1265" data-size="19">工程師直接和客戶溝通，了解到第一手需求</span></li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul>
</li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="70" data-endline="70" data-position="1287" data-size="0">
<p data-position="1285" data-size="0"><span data-position="1287" data-size="22">End to end 的 ownership</span></p>
</li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="71" data-endline="71" data-position="1312" data-size="0">
<p data-position="1310" data-size="0"><span data-position="1312" data-size="16">Pair Programming</span></p>
</li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="72" data-endline="76" data-position="1331" data-size="0">
<p data-position="1329" data-size="0"><span data-position="1331" data-size="21">LLM/AI 時代可以怎麼做，減少認知負債</span></p>
<ul data-position="1357" data-size="0">
<li class="raw" data-startline="73" data-endline="74" data-position="1359" data-size="0"><img src="https://hackmd.io/_uploads/HJatIH5zMe.jpg" alt="PXL_20260625_055923380.MP" class="offline-handled error-handled" data-position="1359" data-size="71" loading="lazy">
<img src="https://hackmd.io/_uploads/HkaYUBqMMe.jpg" alt="PXL_20260625_060112477.MP" class="offline-handled error-handled" data-position="1431" data-size="71" loading="lazy"></li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="75" data-endline="76" data-position="1509" data-size="0"><span data-position="1509" data-size="13">簡單來說就是要記下 ADR</span></li>
</ul>
</li>
</ul><p class="part" data-startline="77" data-endline="77" data-position="1524" data-size="0"><span data-position="1524" data-size="34">AI 只是落實的角色，整體需求、架構或是 flow的設計還是取決在人</span></p><ul class="part" data-startline="78" data-endline="81" data-position="1559" data-size="0">
<li class="raw" data-startline="78" data-endline="79" data-position="1561" data-size="0"><span data-position="1561" data-size="37">設計有信心的 Harness Engineering（人設計，AI 執行）</span>
<blockquote class="raw">
<p data-position="1605" data-size="0"><span data-position="1605" data-size="38">可能比較接近市面上的 Loop Engineering 或 Workflow</span></p>
</blockquote>
</li>
<li class="raw" data-startline="80" data-endline="81" data-position="1646" data-size="0"><span data-position="1646" data-size="19">做完還是要看，但不是全看，是挑重要的看</span></li>
</ul><h2 class="part" data-startline="82" data-endline="82" id="如何衡量認知負債的程度" data-id="如何衡量認知負債的程度"><a class="anchor hidden-xs" href="https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg#%E5%A6%82%E4%BD%95%E8%A1%A1%E9%87%8F%E8%AA%8D%E7%9F%A5%E8%B2%A0%E5%82%B5%E7%9A%84%E7%A8%8B%E5%BA%A6" title="如何衡量認知負債的程度"><span class="octicon octicon-link ph ph-link-simple-horizontal"></span></a><span data-position="1670" data-size="11">如何衡量認知負債的程度</span></h2><p class="part" data-startline="84" data-endline="84" data-position="1683" data-size="0"><strong data-position="1683" data-size="0"><span data-position="1685" data-size="18">如果接手者舉步維艱，代表認知債務極高</span></strong></p><p class="part" data-startline="87" data-endline="87" data-position="1708" data-size="0"><span data-position="1708" data-size="34">Team Topologies 的指標很好用，雖然三叔公不喜歡這本書</span></p><p class="part" data-startline="89" data-endline="89" data-position="1744" data-size="0"><span data-position="1744" data-size="33">LLM/AI 的產生速度超快，但能守意圖和驗證的還是只有人類辦得到</span></p><blockquote class="raw part" data-startline="91" data-endline="91">
<p data-position="1781" data-size="0"><span data-position="1781" data-size="174">"Once, men turned their thinking over to machines in the hope that this would set them free. But that only permitted other men with machines to enslave them." — Frank Herbert</span></p>
</blockquote><p class="part" data-startline="93" data-endline="93" data-position="1957" data-size="0"><span data-position="1957" data-size="17">真正的自由，來自於仍保有的思考能力</span></p><h2 class="part" data-startline="95" data-endline="95" id="討論區" data-id="討論區"><a class="anchor hidden-xs" href="https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg#%E8%A8%8E%E8%AB%96%E5%8D%80" title="討論區"><span class="octicon octicon-link ph ph-link-simple-horizontal"></span></a><span data-position="1979" data-size="3">討論區</span></h2><blockquote class="raw part" data-startline="96" data-endline="96">
<p data-position="1985" data-size="0"><span data-position="1985" data-size="8">歡迎在此進行討論</span></p>
</blockquote><p class="part" data-startline="98" data-endline="98" data-position="1995" data-size="0"><span data-position="1995" data-size="28">因為一個片段看完整部電影 -> 因為一個需求開了整家公司</span></p><blockquote class="raw part" data-startline="100" data-endline="100">
<p data-position="2027" data-size="0"><span data-position="2027" data-size="20">老闆總是試著讓所有成員很忙，感同身受..</span></p>
</blockquote><p class="part" data-startline="102" data-endline="104" data-position="2049" data-size="0"><span data-position="2049" data-size="15">技術課程裡面的勸世文，頗有收穫</span><span class="smartypants" data-position="2064" data-size="3">…</span><br>
<span data-position="2069" data-size="40">希望有機會看到完整的投影片跟無法參與的公司同事分享，不要到時候被AI統治變成奴隸</span><span class="smartypants" data-position="2109" data-size="3">…</span><br>
<span data-position="2114" data-size="11">雖然現在本來就是奴隸了</span><span class="smartypants" data-position="2125" data-size="3">…</span></p><h2 class="part" data-startline="106" data-endline="106" id="相關連結" data-id="相關連結"><a class="anchor hidden-xs" href="https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg#%E7%9B%B8%E9%97%9C%E9%80%A3%E7%B5%90" title="相關連結"><span class="octicon octicon-link ph ph-link-simple-horizontal"></span></a><span data-position="2133" data-size="4">相關連結</span></h2><ul class="part" data-startline="107" data-endline="109" data-position="2138" data-size="0">

Any changes Be notified of any changes - Mention me Be notified of mention me - Unsubscribe # [當AI-帶來思考外包x認知負債，要如何不讓AI-奴役你---柯仁傑-David-Ko敏捷三叔公](https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg\#%E7%95%B6AI-%E5%B8%B6%E4%BE%86%E6%80%9D%E8%80%83%E5%A4%96%E5%8C%85x%E8%AA%8D%E7%9F%A5%E8%B2%A0%E5%82%B5%EF%BC%8C%E8%A6%81%E5%A6%82%E4%BD%95%E4%B8%8D%E8%AE%93AI-%E5%A5%B4%E5%BD%B9%E4%BD%A0---%E6%9F%AF%E4%BB%81%E5%82%91-David-Ko%E6%95%8F%E6%8D%B7%E4%B8%89%E5%8F%94%E5%85%AC "當AI-帶來思考外包x認知負債，要如何不讓AI-奴役你---柯仁傑-David-Ko敏捷三叔公") 當AI 帶來思考外包x認知負債，要如何不讓AI 奴役你 - 柯仁傑 (David Ko/敏捷三叔公) Loading embed note ## [筆記區](https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg\#%E7%AD%86%E8%A8%98%E5%8D%80 "筆記區") 筆記區 ## [GenAI-帶來的認知問題](https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg\#GenAI-%E5%B8%B6%E4%BE%86%E7%9A%84%E8%AA%8D%E7%9F%A5%E5%95%8F%E9%A1%8C "GenAI-帶來的認知問題") GenAI 帶來的認知問題 ![PXL_20260625_053311465.MP (1)](https://hackmd.io/_uploads/r1C9QBqMMe.jpg) > 越來越發散 > 產品越大，人的認知會跟產品全貌有落差 -\> 認知落差 產品程式現狀 <-\> 人對於產品程式現狀，中間的落差 因為 LLM 的增強的關係，開始出現跟不上的內容 > 讓你的思考外包，變笨是唯一的結果？ 刻痕、烙印的重要性 什麼是認知？從接收訊息到做出反應，中間所有的過程 認知負債：沒搞懂但卻立刻就有成品之間的落差 有了AI之後，認知過程就會變了調 > 一杯咖啡，聞不到咖啡香、不知道自己是冰還熱、不知道上次拉肚子了沒、不知道要點冰還熱 ## [傳統錯誤的解法](https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg\#%E5%82%B3%E7%B5%B1%E9%8C%AF%E8%AA%A4%E7%9A%84%E8%A7%A3%E6%B3%95 "傳統錯誤的解法") 傳統錯誤的解法 - 將團隊職責切分得越細，員工需要學習的東西就越少，認知負荷就越低 > 依照「職能」區分團隊 - 案例：導入 Scrum，但卻按職能拆團隊，原本職責廣泛的團隊被分割了 - 需求量大、複雜後，這些小團隊就動不起來了，容易彼此互卡，依賴關係導致交付時間變長 - 軟體組件無法整合，整合DevOps,DevSecOps團隊 - 沒人驗證整體品質，增加QA團隊 - 產品不符需求，增加客戶支援 - 產生踢皮球效應 > 多專案併發與情境切換 - 資源利用率的迷思，為了確保每個組件團隊「有事做」，公司同時啟動多個計畫 - 導致吼叫效應 > 叫得夠大聲才爭得到資源 - 產生許多無效的管理會議 - 分工求生，全端工程師變成孤單工程師 - 把 LLM/AI 當成中樞，把弄懂全局的工作交給 AI - 但這其實也是泰勒(Taylor)的老路 - 可能會付出四個代價 - ![PXL_20260625_055051865.MP (1)](https://hackmd.io/_uploads/Hy6OmrcMfx.jpg) ## [持續學習和回饋才是正解](https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg\#%E6%8C%81%E7%BA%8C%E5%AD%B8%E7%BF%92%E5%92%8C%E5%9B%9E%E9%A5%8B%E6%89%8D%E6%98%AF%E6%AD%A3%E8%A7%A3 "持續學習和回饋才是正解") 持續學習和回饋才是正解 - Spaced Repetition：穩定知識的固著 - ![PXL_20260625_055139230.MP (1)](https://hackmd.io/_uploads/BJwoUS9Mzx.jpg) - 多團隊 Product Backlog Refinement - LeSS 的概念 - 從實例解釋 - ![IMG_8200](https://hackmd.io/_uploads/S1tWXB5zze.jpg) - 傳話遊戲的死穴 - 新角色：Product Developer - 工程師直接和客戶溝通，了解到第一手需求 - End to end 的 ownership - Pair Programming - LLM/AI 時代可以怎麼做，減少認知負債 - ![PXL_20260625_055923380.MP](https://hackmd.io/_uploads/HJatIH5zMe.jpg)![PXL_20260625_060112477.MP](https://hackmd.io/_uploads/HkaYUBqMMe.jpg) - 簡單來說就是要記下 ADR AI 只是落實的角色，整體需求、架構或是 flow的設計還是取決在人 - 設計有信心的 Harness Engineering（人設計，AI 執行） > 可能比較接近市面上的 Loop Engineering 或 Workflow - 做完還是要看，但不是全看，是挑重要的看 ## [如何衡量認知負債的程度](https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg\#%E5%A6%82%E4%BD%95%E8%A1%A1%E9%87%8F%E8%AA%8D%E7%9F%A5%E8%B2%A0%E5%82%B5%E7%9A%84%E7%A8%8B%E5%BA%A6 "如何衡量認知負債的程度") 如何衡量認知負債的程度 **如果接手者舉步維艱，代表認知債務極高** Team Topologies 的指標很好用，雖然三叔公不喜歡這本書 LLM/AI 的產生速度超快，但能守意圖和驗證的還是只有人類辦得到 > "Once, men turned their thinking over to machines in the hope that this would set them free. But that only permitted other men with machines to enslave them." — Frank Herbert 真正的自由，來自於仍保有的思考能力 ## [討論區](https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg\#%E8%A8%8E%E8%AB%96%E5%8D%80 "討論區") 討論區 > 歡迎在此進行討論 因為一個片段看完整部電影 -\> 因為一個需求開了整家公司 > 老闆總是試著讓所有成員很忙，感同身受.. 技術課程裡面的勸世文，頗有收穫… 希望有機會看到完整的投影片跟無法參與的公司同事分享，不要到時候被AI統治變成奴隸… 雖然現在本來就是奴隸了… ## [相關連結](https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg\#%E7%9B%B8%E9%97%9C%E9%80%A3%E7%B5%90 "相關連結") 相關連結 - [DevOpsDays Taipei 2026 官方網站](https://devopsdays.tw/) - [DevOpsDays Taipei 2026 議程表](https://devopsdays.tw/2026/agenda) ###### [tags-DevOpsDays-Taipei-2026-DevOpsDays-Taipei-2026-共筆-2026-共筆-DE-會議室-DevOps新手-Newbie-DevOps老司機-DevOps-Veteran-IT人員--偏開發-IT--DEV-經營決策者-Decision-maker](https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg\#tags-DevOpsDays-Taipei-2026-DevOpsDays-Taipei-2026-%E5%85%B1%E7%AD%86-2026-%E5%85%B1%E7%AD%86-DE-%E6%9C%83%E8%AD%B0%E5%AE%A4-DevOps%E6%96%B0%E6%89%8B-Newbie-DevOps%E8%80%81%E5%8F%B8%E6%A9%9F-DevOps-Veteran-IT%E4%BA%BA%E5%93%A1--%E5%81%8F%E9%96%8B%E7%99%BC-IT--DEV-%E7%B6%93%E7%87%9F%E6%B1%BA%E7%AD%96%E8%80%85-Decision-maker "tags-DevOpsDays-Taipei-2026-DevOpsDays-Taipei-2026-共筆-2026-共筆-DE-會議室-DevOps新手-Newbie-DevOps老司機-DevOps-Veteran-IT人員--偏開發-IT--DEV-經營決策者-Decision-maker") tags: `DevOpsDays Taipei 2026``DevOpsDays Taipei 2026 共筆``2026``共筆``DE 會議室``DevOps新手 (Newbie)``DevOps老司機 (DevOps Veteran)``IT人員 / 偏開發 (IT / DEV)``經營決策者 (Decision maker)` [Table of Contents](https://hackmd.io/WNSBbOJATt62iNShdB5nsg# "Table of Contents") Select a repo Comment

```
See how Anthropic engineers actually use Claude Code day to day. You'll configure a real repo the way we do internally: project context files, custom commands, hooks, and subagents. Then run the same task stock versus tuned and see the difference for yourself.
```

13Likes

16hAgo

Anthropic 的Claude Code 創造者Boris Cherny 在紅杉AI Ascent 2026 上第一次講了他怎麼用loop
他拋出的第一個點：程式設計對他來說已經100% 解決了。 「對我來說，100%。」 不是嘴上說說——他說模型現在寫了他100% 的代碼，每天提幾十個PR，上週單日紀錄是150 個。
Claude Code 的程式碼庫也沒什麼秘密（洩漏了），就是TypeScript 加React。 那他一個人怎麼撐起一天150 個PR？ 靠loop。

這就是最近被反覆討論的那套打法——他讓Claude 用cron 排重複任務，發出去就不管： “而且到現在，我已經有幾十個loop 在跑各種任務了。

” 有一個幫他盯PR、修CI、自動rebase；還有一個每30 分鐘從Twitter 抓反饋幫他聚類。他給的定性很重：「我現在覺得loop 就是未來。」他還提到剛上線的routines，原理一樣，但跑在伺服器上——「就算你關掉筆記本，它也還在繼續跑」。

那這只是他一個人的玩法？ 不是。

他說整個Anthropic 內部「已經沒有任何手寫程式碼了，所有SQL 都是由模型寫的」。工程師各自的Claude 在循環裡跑，也會透過Slack 互相溝通，一起解決問題。…...more

...more

# [谷歌资深工程师断言：代码审查已死！当AI产出翻4倍，程序员如何保住饭碗？](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=j5x5SLW0xGQ)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [Why QQ](https://www.youtube.com/@whycallqq)
**Uploaded at**: 2026-06-17
**Published at**: 
**Length**: 08:33
**Views**: 1794
**Likes**: 63
**Category**: Science & Technology

## Description

```
你有没有发现，自从团队用上了Cursor、Copilot，大家写代码的速度确实快了，但每天Review代码的时间却成倍增加，甚至经常出现没人看就直接Merge的情况？

这绝对不是你一个人的错觉。最近，KTH皇家理工学院的最新论文，加上谷歌资深工程师Addy Osmani的硬核长文，共同揭开了一个残酷的真相：传统的代码审查系统，正在被AI庞大的代码产量彻底压垮。数据显示，AI让代码产出翻了4倍，但审查时间暴涨了441%，更可怕的是，开发者缺陷率从9%飙升到了54%！

本期视频，QQ将带你硬核拆解这场代码审查危机。我们不仅会分析为什么AI写的代码这么难看懂，更会为你提供三套实战级别的应对策略：单人开发怎么搞？初创团队怎么审？企业级屎山代码怎么防？

最后，我还会手把手教你如何用魔法打败魔法——也就是传说中的"用AI审查AI"（Loop Engineering），让机器先帮你过滤掉垃圾代码。点个关注，带你用工程视角看透AI时代的本质，我们马上开始！

https://blog.lightnote.com.cn/google-senior-engineer-code-review-is-dead-ai-4x-output-programmers-career-survival/

#AI编程 #代码审查 #CodeReview #Cursor #Copilot #ClaudeCode #AddyOsmani #软件工程 #程序员 #研发效能 #技术债务 #AI工具 #自动化测试 #GitHub #架构设计 #技术分享 #硬核科普 #编程干货 #研发团队 #技术管理 #代码质量 #AgenticReview #LoopEngineering #多智能体 #开发效率 #技术趋势 #开源社区 #代码规范 #系统架构 #为什么叫QQ
```

# [How This 5x Founder Runs His Startup Solo With AI Agents (OpenClaw, Codex, Devin) | Ryan Carson](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=IDqdVZwAwjw)

**Visibility**: Public
**Uploaded by**: [Peter Yang](https://www.youtube.com/@PeterYangYT)
**Uploaded at**: 2026-05-24
**Published at**: 
**Length**: 39:26
**Views**: 8266
**Likes**: 203
**Category**: Science & Technology

## Description

```
Ryan is a serial founder who’s building his latest startup solo with only AI agents. It was fascinating to watch him demo the exact skills and routines he uses to get OpenClaw, Codex, and Devin to manage his inbox and calendar, do sales outreach, and build features while he sleeps.

Ryan and I talked about:
(00:00) Agents are just cron jobs and text markdown files
(02:25) Demo: How Ryan set up OpenClaw to be his AI chief of staff
(08:14) The setting most people get wrong in OpenClaw
(13:46) How to onboard your agent like you would an employee
(17:06) The cron job that books cold meetings while Ryan sleeps
(24:36) Why Ryan prefers Devin over other AI coding tools
(28:33) Demo: How Ryan ships 10 PRs a day solo
(33:08) Google Ads agencies and a $6K/month designer
(37:11) Why building the system is the real startup work

Thanks to our sponsors:
Oceans: Hire AI-native EAs http://oceanstalent.com/peter
Wispr Flow: Don't type, just speak https://ref.wisprflow.ai/peteryang
Linear: The AI agent platform for modern teams. https://linear.app/behind-the-craft

Get the takeaways: https://creatoreconomy.so/p/how-this-5x-founder-runs-his-startup-solo-with-ai-agents-ryan-carson

Where to find Ryan:
X: https://x.com/ryancarson
Website: https://untangle.us/

Subscribe to this channel — more interviews coming soon!
```

## Transcript

The big thing everybody needs to
remember about agents is that they are
cron jobs and markdown files. [music]
I'm probably shipping at least 10 PRs a
day, sometimes a lot more. It's almost
easier to onboard and train agents than

coverage. So, this is an automation that
uh
that literally runs the entire user
experience
through my app from sign up to
completing a divorce case and it does it
automatically once a week. And the way
this works is it uses a playbook.
And again, so this is kind of I just
want people to think whether you use
Devin or not, I think the idea of having
automations that use playbooks and then
increase and then uh making those
playbooks better and better is a very
powerful uh mechanism to use. What what
is a playbook? Yeah. So a playbook is
similar to a skill. Um
so what this is telling uh Devin how to
do is how to do this entire uh
run-through process. So it's like what
what's needed from the user, okay, read
the skill, set up everything. It's just
how to do it and so that is being used
by this automation every week. So it's
uh it's like using some playwright kind
of thing to click through? Yeah. And
that's the other thing about Devin
that's just amazing is like the browser
testing just works. So the agent can use
its own browser in the cloud
absolutely reliably and
deterministically. Got it. So those are
just like cron jobs, right? Like they're
they're basically just These are cron
jobs with skills attached. Like I said,
the whole thing with agents is cron jobs
and skills, right? Um so if you can nail
that down, um you know, a couple others
like I do a lot of these smoke tests
where it's like sign up and use this
feature in the app and make sure it
works. Record your screen and then tell
me if anything breaks. We're doing all
that. All right, so that is my
engineering. I use Devin, I love it. Um
I think it's
uh I think it's
I think it's state of the art. So I I do
a lot of this. Now,
as you know, GPT-55 just came out

good in Codex, right? So I'm going to
share my Codex screen. Uh let me ask you
this while you share screen.
Since you're a solo founder, do you even
do PRs and stuff? Like how do you review
your code? Okay, so what you'll find is
that if you have a good code factory,
and what I mean by code factory is that
agents are writing as close to 100% of
the code as as possible and reviewing
and shipping 100% of the code. If you
actually want to do that, you need to
actually have a software development
life cycle that feels like it's designed
for a very large team. Because what you
find is that your agents are just like
humans and they need reproducible uh
processes that don't rely on you, right?
And so PRs are absolutely key to that.
So like if we go um
So everything is a PR.
Um what I do is is when a PR is ready to
ship, so it's actually working on some
stuff. Um
So I have this playbook called land PR.
And land PR basically explains to Devin
exactly how to review, resolve, um and
then merge a PR without me.
Got it.
And I use this probably, I mean, 10 to
20 times a day. And I I really don't
look at code anymore. I mean,
maybe occasionally.
Um
but I'm reading a lot of PRs. I'm
reading a lot of um I wouldn't even say
I read the PRs as much as I read the
markdown and I read what the agent is
saying to me about it. Okay, Ryan. So um
I'd love to hear just like end-to-end,
uh now that you're solo founder, how you
ship a new feature for Untangled. Yeah,
like what do you use to plan? What do
you use to ship? Yep. So um I start in
Devin. Um and you know, I've spent a lot
of time customizing that environment. So
there's uh there's a lot of agents that
MD files, there's a lot of knowledge,
there's a lot of skills. So I think
everybody has to start there, right? So
when now that that's set up properly,
then I I go in and I basically use
WhisperFlow like everybody. And I just
talk for a while. And I'll say, "Okay,
you know, here's everything I'm thinking
about this feature." And I just brain
dump. And then I'll say, "Help me make a
PRD."
Um and then I have a skill um then it's
not rocket science, but it's basically
how to turn something into a PRD.
And uh really all that is is is
encouraging the agent to ask me
questions and clarify. And so we'll go
through that process for a while, and
then I'll ask it to write uh the PRD as
a markdown file. And then I'll say,
"Build it." And the interesting thing is
I I think you know with Codex or Devin
or some of the tools, they're starting
to do auto compaction pretty well now.
So I actually work in one thread. And I
know there's auto compaction happening
magically in the background, but I don't
really have to think about, you know,
stopping a thread and restarting
anymore. I think the really good agent
harnesses do that for you now, and it's
pretty it's pretty good.
Um
and then honestly, you know, good tools
like a Devin or a Codex or uh I don't
use Cloud Code. I haven't used Cloud
Code in a long time. Um
uh but I think between Codex and Devin,
I honestly I'll walk away at that point,
you know, or I'll completely switch uh
context to something else.
And you know, I'll let that uh you know,
work for 10 20 minutes, and then I'll
come back. I think the other important
thing is the the reason why I ended up
going with Devin is because of the
testing feature. So it has a really good
feedback loop where the agent can use a
browser and try the feature. And this is
what I tried to set up locally where
you're trying to get the browser to
work, you're trying to get the feedback
loop to work. It was hard, whereas it's
just all built in in Devin. And so it
will it will do a screencast, record it,

with the with the its own PR, and then

back. Uh and then I'll do some feedback,

land PR skill, and then and then ship

and I'm probably shipping at least 10

sometimes a lot more.

and in addition to I mean, you don't
just build stuff. You have to do

so, I don't use the Google Ads UI at

about it.
>> Yeah, probably not that good in the

very complex. You know, you used to have
to hire these agency to do your Google
Ads, right? Because it's so freaking
complex. And the great thing about using
the agent is it can deal with that
complexity. You use it to set up the ads
for you, too, or just read the stats?
Everything. So, it it has It's basically
has upsert capability. So, it can it can
insert, update, delete any campaign. And
that sounds amazing, but all I did is
talk to Devin about it and, you know,
built the CLI, gave it an API key, uh
created an app, which you have to do,
and then bada bing. And how about the I
was going to ask you about the ad
creative and also the designs for your
product, right? Okay, so I think we all
know this now. Image 2, I think is the

um Open AI image model. It's just so
good. I mean, it's so so good. And so, I
have a
a workflow where, you know, content
creation is obviously part of the key
here for driving organic traffic. So,
what I do is I go out and find an expert
to interview.
Um, I use Descript to do that. And then,
what I'll do is I'll quickly chop that
uh interview up into 60-second segments.
And then, the key is I upload those to a
uh a Google Drive.
And they're just MP4s.
And then, what I did is I set up, again,
a uh a playbook in Devin to say, "Every
night, see if there is MP4s in that
folder.
I want you to make an API call." I use
Gemini for this. Um,
uh make a call to Gemini, watch the
video,
and write a description of it. And then,
I want um the new openAI model, image
model, to create me a really nice cover
image for that video.
Um, and so, I've got a nice cover image
cuz, as you know, that's like the whole
key. Then, it writes the copy with
Gemini. And then, I publish it all to
social using Publer. And Publer is just
a has a good API for publishing to
social. So, this is the machine, right?
Like, you're trying to build the
machine, right? And then, eventually,
I'll hire someone to run marketing.
But, I want to know how to do it and
operate the machine myself first and
make sure that it works well. And you're
able to get the brand consistency uh
from like just some prompts? It's crazy.
Yeah, you know, it it there's there's a
set up cost, right? So, essentially,
what I've done, and and this is similar
with Claude design, which we're all
playing with right now. The idea is to
create a design.md
um and some reference images, right? So,
what I I've actually paid a designer,
his name is Brett from Design Joy. He
costs about six grand a month.
Um, I had him do all the initial
branding work and all of the initial
social images. So, I have a library of
good of good image. And now what I do is
I use those as reference images with a
very good design at MD, and then I find
that the new image model from OpenAI is
completely able to reproduce it. All
right, you just saved $6,000 a month, I
guess. That's good.
>> [laughter]
>> Basically, I mean, you know, I think
it's important to pay a designer to do
the first cut, right? I I we're very
much in a world where you need the taste
and experience of a very good designer
to build out your brand, your layout,
and some basic social images, but after
that, I think I think honestly we're in
a world now where you can use Cloud
Design plus
OpenAI's new image model to get
unlimited, you know, perfectly branded
imagery. It's wild. Awesome, man. So, so
I guess the takeaway from this interview
is like
I feel like a lot of the work we do
these days is just like setting up the
system to do the work, right? Is that a
good takeaway from this interview? Or It
is. Take the time to set up the system
to do the work, because then, number
one, you are
understanding how the work happens, and
you're refining it. And then, you either
bring on more agents to augment that, or
you bring on a human that's augmented
with agents to do that. But it's funny,
cuz I think we used to in startups, we
used to say just do the bare minimum to
get the MVP out. Like
do not spend time on systems or
processes or documentation.
But it's literally the reverse now. So,
now, as a startup founder, you have to
spend a lot of time to set up your
software development life cycle, your
documentation,
your reference images, build all that
into a cron job with a with skill file,
and then you find that, you know, then
you suddenly are unlocked, and you're
doing the work of 10 people. But the
setup is pretty brutal. I feel like,
especially for your startup, maybe most
of your data and is freed up to talk to
other humans and like, you know, do the
do the more human stuff, right?
Yep. Yeah. I mean, you know, I had it's
interesting because I built Untangle um
before we raised money. You know, I
built basically the MVP um in my free
time and and so the product was
basically built and now it's all about
go to market. Um so I spent a lot of
time thinking about marketing, uh which
is fun. I think working companies tend
to bifurcate between product people and
marketing people and you got to do both,
man. You can't just do one of it. Now,
you need to do it all. Like you need to
think about well, which features will uh
unlock revenue and then how do I quickly
Building them is almost trivial now and
now it's issue of go to market and
adoption and product-led growth and like
that's the skill is is it's almost
everybody is a founder now uh even even
if you're not. Awesome, Ryan. Thank you
so much for sharing so much uh
information and also like resources for
free online. And um I guess people can
find you on Twitter at Is it Ryan
Carson? Yep. Any I'm everywhere as Ryan
Carson, so just Google Ryan Carson and
you will find me.
>> [laughter]
>> Okay, cool. All right, man. Well, you're
you're personally an inspiration to me,
so thanks so much for chatting again.
Thanks for having me on. Take care.
Cool.

items.author: Paul CH
items.content_html:
Post by @MaxBrodeurUrbas

Author: Max Brodeur-Urbas @MaxBrodeurUrbas Posted: 2026-05-27T20:38:46.000Z URL: https://x.com/MaxBrodeurUrbas/status/2059736090937081980 Likes: 229 | Retweets: 19

Post

We use agents for everything at Gumloop. This is not a brag. We're seeing efficiency boosts but it's also causing a whole new set of problems. We're experience "agentic growing pains" that a more standard company might only see in 6 months to a year from now. The most concerning issue is how easy it’s becoming to let agents get in between a person and their craft. The thing that made them exceptional before AI.

If you’re not making a conscious effort to stay human and close to your craft, you will slip. Using AI for everything is the path of least resistance once you have it connected to everything. We’ve been jokingly calling it the “slippery slop(e)”.

I put out a short memo in Slack a few weeks ago about it and thought I should convert it to a longer post.

Hey everyone

I wanted to flag something that Rahul and I were chatting about so that we can bake it into some sort of company policy or mantra.

Resist the slop, we win by staying human.

We’ve seen this issue come up a few times in certain customer interactions, in the way we’ve seen agents being used to communicate internally and in how some tasks are getting done in general.

  1. If something takes less time for you to create than for the recipient to consume, it’s probably slop

Creating content is unbelievably easy now. Brevity and clarity is all that matters. That is where the craft is.

I do not care if AI can make a 40 page slide deck for a presentation if the content could have been a single slack message. Time is so precious. Do not waste other people's time asking them to consume something you put minimal effort into.

Marrying AI content generation with high effort creativity is the magic. AI should shorten your journey to exceptional output instead of teleporting you to mediocrity.

  1. If our support doesn't feel deeply human, we're failing.

Everyone on the team provides support in one way or another so this is relevant to everyone. If we’re engaging in a support thread, the customer is already confused or annoyed. The single worst thing we can do is make them feel like they’re talking to a bot.

There was this old programming interview question I loved where you’d ask “write an algorithm to determine who won a tennis match when given all individual game scores”. Everyone would try to make an algo that counts points and attributes games to each player when the real answer is simple. If you just look at the last point scored, that’s who won.

You can read a support thread the same way. I like to scan customer channels, open threads and look at the last message. If it’s “thank you so much it worked great” I close it happy. If there’s no reply or a hanging “let me know if that works for you” from our side, I get the strong feeling the customer is not happy. Just because they went away does not mean they're satisfied.

Not replying to a thread is a very intentional act from the customer if they went so far out of their way to start that support thread. Using AI to reply will always be the easiest way to send the ball over the net but it does not make them want to play. They will realize they’re playing with a machine and will walk away. We’ll lose customers this way and entire accounts can die as a result.

  1. Internally, people want to talk to you, not your agent.

If you’re using an agent to interact with people on the team, you’re most likely wasting their time.

We specifically hired everyone on the team because we value working directly with you as people. Having your agent reply to coworkers messages is often times worse than not replying at all.

Pairing AI with the fact that everyone on this team is fucking cracked is how we win.

Top Comments

1. @taarichandaria

“If something takes less time for you to create than for the recipient to consume, it’s probably slop”

Banger

Likes: 3

3. @KanyesThaker

Author: kanyes Posted: 2026-05-28T02:22:37.000Z URL: https://x.com/KanyesThaker/status/2059822623174050112

the product itself needs to be designed around this constraint though (long-term). can't rely on the user to police themselves. it's a hard problem, but i think very doable to design agents that are empowering without agency-draining

Likes: 2

4. @BradleyYoungjr

Author: Brad Posted: 2026-05-28T04:29:18.000Z URL: https://x.com/BradleyYoungjr/status/2059854503973724649

Man this was really refreshing to read. When consumption takes longer than creation 🔥

Likes: 1

5. @emtalas

items.content_html:
‹ home
Table of Contents
› How I use LLMs Now
› Modern Engineering Values
› Strong Ownership
› Taste, Taste, Taste
› Strict Guardrails & Fast Feedback Loops
› Context in the Repo
› Own your Stack
› Option Value
› On Management
› How much faster?

Modern Engineering Values

Published on June 3, 2026
14 minutes reading time, 2700 words

At the end of last year I shared my LLM workflow in You are absolutely right!?. I knew it would be outdated quickly, but I didn’t realize it would be outdated that quickly.

I actually cannot believe that I rarely write code by hand anymore. Or rather, I cannot believe that I used to write code by hand! Programming has fundamentally changed and I’ve been wondering which engineering values still matter.

Before I get started, and to avoid criticism that I haven’t shipped anything, here are my contributions over the past several months:

  • Vite+: Built features in Rust for the launch (I don’t even know Rust), 90% AI-written
  • fate 1.0: Added live views, drizzle & GraphQL support, a Vite plugin, and garbage collection, 100% AI-written
  • Codiff: A fast, minimal, and beautiful diff review app, 100% AI-written
  • Athena Crisis: New features and 70+ bugfixes, 100% AI-written
  • Void: Metaframework and Cloud platform (Not yet shipped), 100% AI-written

Overall, I’ve found that coding agents now write code that is as good as or better than what I would write, and they do it in minutes instead of weeks. Since coding is no longer the bottleneck, I get a lot of work done that otherwise wouldn’t have happened at all1I even get to do silly things, like “VoidZero plays Pokémon”, a custom ROM hack that worked over websockets, in about five prompts, for our internal All Hands.

Most of my work is public and you can validate my work directly. I’m not bragging, I’m simply astonished. There has never been a developer experience improvement of this magnitude that allowed me to ship higher quality software so much faster.

Take Athena Crisis for example: It’s a pre-AI codebase that I wrote by hand. I haven’t had time to work on it lately so I had Codex run in a loop to identify bugs and fix them. It fixed 70 bugs, implemented new features, and wrote tests – and I was able to do all that while I was waiting on agents in other projects! As a result, Athena Crisis is now faster, more stable, and has more features than ever before. On top of all that it streamlined CI steps, improved the Steam app upload pipeline, wrote tests for some complex multi-user interactions, and I had it make a custom app to compare 200 pixel art icons with new variants to give the game a new look. None of this would have happened without coding agents.

How I use LLMs Now

Last year I didn’t run models locally, mostly isolated context to a specific module or function, and generated multiple versions for each solution.

Now, I’m using the Codex CLI, with GPT 5.5 high.2I prefer the CLI because it starts with a clean slate. There is no sidebar with old chats I don’t need, and I prefer one window per project instead of all projects in one window. It consistently one-shots correct solutions with very little prompting or structure, as long as the guardrails in the codebase are strong. I found that lower reasoning results in poor quality, and xhigh is too slow and overcomplicates things. I tried various other models, but nothing is as fast, as correct, or as easy to work with as Codex.3I appreciate everyone working on local models, and have high hopes for all of them to converge eventually!

I am ineffective at running multiple agent sessions in the same project (with worktrees, copies, or other solutions). I still review all code, and juggling orthogonal code changes in the same project slows me down. Context switching allows me to scale myself better and I can effectively work on multiple projects simultaneously. I usually have one Ghostty window per project, with a Codex tab on the left and a terminal on the right. Now I can work on 3-6 projects at a time, and the bottleneck is usually discussing and reviewing code.

All this multitasking added a spatial dimension to my work. I used to love just working from anywhere using my laptop, but now I prefer having a large screen available and assigning parts of the screen to various projects. For example, fate always ends up in the top center of the screen, void on the left side, and Athena Crisis on the right. Somehow the spatial assignment helps me multitask more effectively.

In terms of prompts, I’m pretty lazy. I often start new sessions for new topics (although context management matters much less with Codex these days) and ask “I want to build X. Get context from Y and Z, make a proposal, and ask me any questions you might have”. I iterate on the proposal by talking to the agent before asking it to execute on the plan.

When fixing bugs, I point it at a repro or context and force it to first create a failing test. This significantly increases the likelihood of the model fixing the right problem in the right way. Otherwise models tend to fix the wrong thing, make the wrong fix, or write the wrong test.

Keeping models on a tight leash is still the best method to get good or great solutions out of them. They still go off the rails and make many mistakes, and sometimes it’s better to kill the session and start from scratch. For larger or more complex changes I run a few /review cycles focusing on different areas to make sure the code is high-quality, bug free, and matches the plan. Once I validate the functionality (visually, functional, or with tests, etc.) I review the code using Codiff4Install via brew install --cask nkzw-tech/tap/codiff!, usually by typing codiff -w which uses Codex to create a walkthrough of the uncommitted changes. I sometimes ship code that I don’t study closely – if the tests look good, the code structure looks fine, and is in a less critical area that can be changed easily. In general, agents don’t make the type of mistakes humans used to make, and at this point I put more scrutiny on hand-written code than on agent-written code.

The best next step is to push to main. Unfortunately, that’s not how most teams operate, so once you enter the PR or collaboration phase you kind of lose most of the velocity that LLMs give you. I believe that all technical organizations will have to drastically change and break down barriers to getting things done if they want to be successful going forward.

Modern Engineering Values

I’ve been trying to wrap my head around engineering values and principles that matter for 2026 and beyond that will help individuals and organizations move faster. Looking at my engineering workflow it feels like on one hand everything changed, and on the other, not that much.

I thought about how individual people operate and how it differs from big tech. A lot of infrastructure that small teams are building now is similar to the type of infrastructure that big tech has had for many years. It’s funny when people call out how much slop agents are producing. They must have never seen the slop engineers produce at big tech! Other people call out: How can you be effective when you don’t understand the code? Guess what, at big tech, most people don’t understand much of the code.

I don’t think programming or code will go away. Markdown files with specs written in English is a terrible programming language full of ambiguity. Dynamic code with agents seems like a much more sensible approach. Engineering is becoming less about producing code and more about directing systems that produce code. Things will stay closer to what they are than most people think.

So how do you move fast and what are the values that matter? Regardless of whether the engineers are human or AI, I think the values are very similar:

Strong Ownership

The best engineers exhibit strong ownership and domain expertise in the area they work in. Agents amplify ownership. Someone who deeply understands a product can now execute faster, while someone without context can generate much more noise. As a result, coordination becomes more expensive. I believe the most effective teams will be small, often two to three people, with clear ownership boundaries and isolated repositories.5This is painful to me as I was always a big proponent of monorepos and helped bring workspaces to JavaScript!

Strong ownership means understanding the architecture, product requirements, tradeoffs, and long-term direction of a system. It means reviewing the work produced by agents, making decisions confidently, and sometimes pushing directly to main.

Code reviews on small teams should focus on alignment, not on arguments about code. They should only be necessary when there are open questions that haven’t already been discussed.

Taste, Taste, Taste

I have a low tolerance for bullshit, and with agents, everyone can generate a lot of bullshit all day long. Please just stop. Good taste is not just about building great products, it’s about what makes it worth spending your time on something in the first place. I recommend engineering teams to spend more time on figuring out what is worth spending time on.

Strict Guardrails & Fast Feedback Loops

To keep velocity up, large organizations tend to put heavy constraints on what code should look like. Working with coding agents is basically the same as working in a large organization: You drop new engineers without context on the codebase all the time, just like agents! The more constraints like lint rules, automated testing, and other methods of fast verification you put on the code, the faster people can iterate on their changes, and the faster they can get work done.

Fast and scalable tooling matters even more with agents than before. Tighter feedback loops make it faster for agents to iterate and complete their work. Because the amount of code produced is accelerating, it’s important for tools to operate on changed files instead of the whole repo and you don’t want tools to become slower as the lines of code grow.

Strict guardrails and fast feedback loops are the difference between an agent completing their work in 1 minute or 60.

Context in the Repo

There was a recent argument (or joke?) about how tests are the context, or how design docs are the context, and how it might be worth keeping that context “secret” from other parties. The big realization is that in the past, context was scattered across many places:

  • Notion / Google Docs
  • People
  • Implicit context (Not written down anywhere or forgotten)
  • How the product actually behaves in production
  • Commit messages
  • Code

Since each agent session is like a new employee, likely without access to all these places to gather context, the best thing is to put all the relevant context together, locally, in the repo. This is a great place to inject taste, values, principles and your own thinking. Do not let agents put slop all over your codebase. The great thing about doing this is that it will make the codebase more accessible for agents and humans both!

Similarly, while we might look at individual lines of code less than we used to, there is even more value in making sure code is optimized for reading. Simpler and less code is easier to understand and fix.

Own your Stack

Historically, you might have built apps where 95% of the code was third-party dependencies. It made sense to depend on external libraries because writing and maintaining code was slow and expensive. However, it also locked you into a stack you didn’t control. The data fetching library or third-party UI framework might have dictated the downstream user experience.

Every dependency introduces code, architecture, constraints, and decisions that you are ultimately responsible for, regardless of who wrote them. They make it feel like you’re shipping a small application when in reality you’re shipping a large body of code that you didn’t write and may not fully understand. Agents change these cost factors and reduce complexity.

Over the past several years I built my own open source JavaScript stack around data libraries, i18n, UI libraries, tool configs, starter templates, slideshows and more. It allows me to spin up new projects with tight constraints for agents quickly, and more importantly, it gives me full control over the product and user experience. With agents, there is no longer a good reason to outsource core parts of your product to third-party dependencies if you can realistically own them yourself.

Option Value

I wrote about Option Value in Principles of Developer Experience:

[This principle] is about retaining or gaining option value, which means any change to a system should unlock more options for improvements and significant future changes. […] If we keep option value in mind when redesigning infrastructure, we can naturally adapt to new requirements in the future.

AI fundamentally changes how long it takes to make large-scale changes. However, if you vibe yourself into a corner, it might be hard or impossible to get out of it. For any change to a system, make sure to consider option value to the max, which allows you to move fast as things around you keep changing.

On Management

I have been reflecting on what modern engineering management looks like now. There were fundamental human limits to team sizes, scope, and management layers before. What we are seeing now, and what we’ll continue to see, is these limits being pushed. Some companies will get this wrong in one way or another in the short-term, but directionally I believe that engineering management is going to become more technical, not less. Of course, in most tech companies, the engineering managers are or used to be technical already. However, many of them, especially once they enter middle management, tend to lose touch with “the code”.

As execution becomes cheaper and individual contributors gain more leverage, managers can no longer just own outcomes or direction. They have to retain domain expertise in the area they work in, be able to make changes to their projects confidently, and provide technical leadership. I wrote about this years ago in Tech Lead Management, and I’m convinced this role is the right one for most managers in the agent era.

How much faster?

Ok, but how much faster can you move with agents? Looking at my GitHub contributions in the past 30 days, I pushed 770 commits and modified 15k lines of code on average each day (312k added, 154k removed, including days I didn’t code). Compared to the same time a year earlier, I shipped 328 commits, and modified about 5k lines of code each day (3k added, 2k removed). 2024 looks roughly similar to 2025. This means I made more than twice as many commits, and I shipped 3 times as much code as in the same period two years prior.6Of course, commits and lines of code are normally a bad metric, but I’m comparing my productivity to my past self, and I have roughly worked on projects in the same space.

My best days when writing code by hand were about 1200 lines a day. I had like one of those a week. Now I ship ten times more, at a higher quality. It’s more intense, faster, and addictive – but I genuinely feel like I move fast with stable infrastructure.

People are concerned about their jobs. Sometimes I feel like I’m just carrying context from one agent session into a GitHub Issue, or into Discord, and back to the agent. However, unless models receive innate motivation aligned with my goals and taste it is unlikely that I’ll be cut out of this process anytime soon.

In fact, I feel like the opposite is true. We are unlocking the ability to ship amazing things at lightning speed, and we need more people than ever to do it. I was bottlenecked on writing code, now I’m bottlenecked on exercising judgement. Let’s ship!

Thanks for reading.

# Modern Engineering Values

Publishedon June 3, 2026

14 minutes reading time, 2700 words

At the end of last year I shared my LLM workflow in [You are absolutely right!?](https://cpojer.net/posts/you-are-absolutely-right). I knew it would be outdated quickly, but I didn’t realize it would be outdated _that quickly_.

I actually cannot believe that I rarely write code by hand anymore. Or rather, **I cannot believe that I used to write code by hand!** Programming has fundamentally changed and I’ve been wondering which engineering values still matter.

* * *

Before I get started, and to avoid criticism that I haven’t shipped anything, here are my contributions over the past several months:

- [Vite+](https://viteplus.dev/): Built features in Rust for the launch (I don’t even know Rust), _90% AI-written_
- [_fate_ 1.0](https://fate.technology/posts/fate-1.0): Added live views, drizzle & GraphQL support, a Vite plugin, and garbage collection, _100% AI-written_
- [Codiff](https://github.com/nkzw-tech/codiff): A fast, minimal, and beautiful diff review app, _100% AI-written_
- [Athena Crisis](https://athenacrisis.com/): New features and 70+ bugfixes, _100% AI-written_
- [Void](https://void.cloud/): Metaframework and Cloud platform (Not yet shipped), _100% AI-written_

Overall, I’ve found that coding agents now write code that is as good as or better than what I would write, and they do it in minutes instead of weeks. Since coding is no longer the bottleneck, I get a lot of work done that otherwise _wouldn’t have happened at all_ 1I even get to do silly things, like “VoidZero plays Pokémon”, a custom ROM hack that worked over websockets, in about five prompts, for our internal All Hands.

Most of my work is public and you can validate my work directly. I’m not bragging, I’m simply astonished. There has never been a developer experience improvement of this magnitude that allowed me to ship higher quality software so much faster.

Take Athena Crisis for example: It’s a pre-AI codebase that I wrote by hand. I haven’t had time to work on it lately so I had Codex run in a loop to identify bugs and fix them. It fixed 70 bugs, implemented new features, and wrote tests – and I was able to do all that while I was waiting on agents in other projects! As a result, Athena Crisis is now faster, more stable, and has more features than ever before. On top of all that it streamlined CI steps, improved the Steam app upload pipeline, wrote tests for some complex multi-user interactions, and I had it make a custom app to compare 200 pixel art icons with new variants to give the game a new look. None of this would have happened without coding agents.

## [How I use LLMs Now](https://cpojer.net/posts/modern-engineering-values\#how-i-use-llms-now)

Last year I didn’t run models locally, mostly isolated context to a specific module or function, and generated multiple versions for each solution.

Now, I’m using the Codex CLI, with GPT 5.5 high.2I prefer the CLI because it starts with a clean slate. There is no sidebar with old chats I don’t need, and I prefer one window per project instead of all projects in one window. It consistently one-shots correct solutions with very little prompting or structure, as long as the guardrails in the codebase are strong. I found that lower reasoning results in poor quality, and xhigh is too slow and overcomplicates things. I tried various other models, but nothing is as fast, as correct, or as easy to work with as Codex.3I appreciate everyone working on local models, and have high hopes for all of them to converge eventually!

I am ineffective at running multiple agent sessions in the same project (with worktrees, copies, or other solutions). I still review all code, and juggling orthogonal code changes in the same project slows me down. Context switching allows me to scale myself better and I can effectively work on multiple projects simultaneously. I usually have one Ghostty window per project, with a Codex tab on the left and a terminal on the right. Now I can work on 3-6 projects at a time, and the bottleneck is usually discussing and reviewing code.

All this multitasking added a spatial dimension to my work. I used to love just working from anywhere using my laptop, but now I prefer having a large screen available and assigning parts of the screen to various projects. For example, fate always ends up in the top center of the screen, void on the left side, and Athena Crisis on the right. Somehow the spatial assignment helps me multitask more effectively.

In terms of prompts, I’m pretty lazy. I often start new sessions for new topics (although context management matters much less with Codex these days) and ask “I want to build X. Get context from Y and Z, make a proposal, and ask me any questions you might have”. I iterate on the proposal by talking to the agent before asking it to execute on the plan.

When fixing bugs, I point it at a repro or context and force it to first create a failing test. This significantly increases the likelihood of the model fixing the _right problem in the right way_. Otherwise models tend to fix the wrong thing, make the wrong fix, or write the wrong test.

Keeping models on a tight leash is still the best method to get good or great solutions out of them. They still go off the rails and make many mistakes, and sometimes it’s better to kill the session and start from scratch. For larger or more complex changes I run a few `/review` cycles focusing on different areas to make sure the code is high-quality, bug free, and matches the plan. Once I validate the functionality (visually, functional, or with tests, etc.) I review the code using [Codiff](https://github.com/nkzw-tech/codiff) 4Install via `brew install --cask nkzw-tech/tap/codiff`!, usually by typing `codiff -w` which uses Codex to create a walkthrough of the uncommitted changes. I sometimes ship code that I don’t study closely – if the tests look good, the code structure looks fine, and is in a less critical area that can be changed easily. In general, agents don’t make the type of mistakes humans used to make, and at this point I put more scrutiny on hand-written code than on agent-written code.

The best next step is to push to `main`. Unfortunately, that’s not how most teams operate, so once you enter the PR or collaboration phase you kind of lose most of the velocity that LLMs give you. ### [Option Value](https://cpojer.net/posts/modern-engineering-values\#option-value)

I wrote about Option Value in [Principles of Developer Experience](https://cpojer.net/posts/principles-of-devx#5-maximize-option-value):

items.content_html:
Post by @patrick_oshag

Author: Patrick OShaughnessy @patrick_oshag Posted: 2026-05-13T12:00:02.000Z URL: https://x.com/patrick_oshag/status/2054532117410054252 Likes: 6,810 | Retweets: 803

Post

Krishna Rao is the CFO of Anthropic, and this is his first podcast appearance.

He joined the company two years ago when run-rate revenue was about $250M. Today it is $30B. He has helped raise ~$75B and is responsible for the procurement and allocation of compute.

I feel lucky we get to hear what it is like to sit inside a company this consequential at a moment this pivotal.

We discuss:

  • The cone of uncertainty
  • How he allocates compute across Trainium, TPUs, and GPUs
  • What investors misunderstand about model companies
  • Why the returns to frontier intelligence keep rising
  • Platform vs application and where Anthropic builds its own products
  • How Anthropic uses Claude internally

I have asked my closing question about the kindest thing more than 500 times. Krishna's answer is one I have never heard before.

Enjoy!

Timestamps: 0:00 Intro 2:38 The Compute Canvas 6:51 The "Cone of Uncertainty" 11:58 Why the Returns to Frontier Intelligence Are So High 16:45 Recursive Self-Improvement 20:20 Scaling Laws 23:30 Sourcing $100 Billion in Compute 28:05 Platform vs. Application Strategy 32:52 Pricing Dynamics 38:48 How Anthropic’s Finance Team Uses Claude 43:24 Raising Capital & Overcoming Investor Skepticism 52:32 Public Perception, Risks, and Government Regulation 57:25 Mythos Release 1:12:33 What Could Derail the AI Revolution? 1:13:47 Biotech and Healthcare 1:15:31 The Kindest Thing

# Post by @patrick\_oshag

URL: [https://x\.com/patrick\_oshag/status/2054532117410054252](https://x.com/patrick_oshag/status/2054532117410054252)
Likes: 6,810 | Retweets: 803

## Post

Krishna Rao is the CFO of Anthropic, and this is his first podcast appearance.

He joined the company two years ago when run-rate revenue was about $250M. Today it is $30B. He has helped raise ~$75B and is responsible for the procurement and allocation of compute.

I feel lucky we get to hear what it is like to sit inside a company this consequential at a moment this pivotal.

We discuss:
- The cone of uncertainty
- How he allocates compute across Trainium, TPUs, and GPUs
- What investors misunderstand about model companies
- Why the returns to frontier intelligence keep rising
- Platform vs application and where Anthropic builds its own products
- How Anthropic uses Claude internally

I have asked my closing question about the kindest thing more than 500 times. Krishna's answer is one I have never heard before.

Enjoy!

Timestamps:
0:00 Intro
2:38 The Compute Canvas
6:51 The "Cone of Uncertainty" 
11:58 Why the Returns to Frontier Intelligence Are So High

I think [_a lot_](https://jakub.kr/) about what makes an interface feel great.

When you go from using a good product to a great one, you can _feel_ the difference. It's often hard to point to what makes it though.

It's usually not a single thing, but instead a lot of small things that add up.

In the age of artificial intelligence, shipping fast is no longer an advantage. When anything can be replicated in minutes, just building something _isn't_ enough.

Products that are going to stand out and last are those that are built extremely well, with intent and extraordinary _care_.

That's what _Interfaces_ focuses on.

## About Interfaces

I love writing and sharing what I learn. Articles on my website have been read more thanone million times. It makes me very happy that people find them useful.

Interfaces is where I shareeverything I knowand continue to learn about building great interfaces.

I cover a variety of topics from animation and typography to layout, color and everything else that is a part of building a _great interface_.

items.og_description:
如何把「善用 AI」寫到履歷中?
󠀠
因為近期使用 AI 這項能力逐漸成為軟體工程師的標配，許多公司在找新員工時，也會評估對 AI 工具使用的精熟程度。在 ExplainThis 經營的 E+ 社群當中，也有許多讀者陸續在履歷上面寫跟使用 AI 相關的列點。
󠀠
就寫列點來說，如果要讓看履歷的人感受到「這個候選人善用 AI」，核心關鍵是要寫出相關的關鍵字 (所以 ATS 能抓到)，以及要寫出成果影響力 (所以用人主管會想找來面試)。
󠀠
公司在評估工程師時，不只會問有沒有用過 AI 工具 (現在幾乎多數公司都預設工程師有在用)，而是會進一步評估「怎麼用? 多有效?」。換句話說，有沒有將 AI 運用到多個環節、用了後產生多大的影響力，這些都會是評估的方向。因此，不能只是寫一句「熟悉 AI 工具」而已，而是讓人看見你怎麼把 AI 變成工作流程的一部分，並且有實際的成果。
󠀠
如果要進一步區分初階與資深工程師使用 AI 的方式，那麼只停留在用 AI 寫程式、寫測試，這種最基本的列點，會比較難與初階工程師拉開區別。能否在用 AI 時對接到適當的外部工具、能否透過代理技能 (agent skills) 來提升整個團隊的品質與一致性、能否透過子代理來加速、能否在用 AI 的同時最小化 tokens 消耗成本，都會是評估的面向。
󠀠
即使這些平常都有做到，如果在呈現上沒有修飾，也可能會看起來沒區別度。以下我們會透過三個實際列點，來談可以如何寫更好。
󠀠
範例一「會用 AI 做 Code Review」
󠀠
首先，如果履歷上寫「使用 AI 協助 Code Review，提升程式碼品質」，這會讓人知道你懂得用 AI 來把關程式碼品質，但這幾乎是人人都能寫出的點。
󠀠
更理想的寫法會是 「設計複利工程工作流程，讓 AI 代理自動萃取每次互動的模式並融入技能庫，使 Code Review 重複性意見減少 XX%，審核來回次數從 XX 次降至 YY 次」。
󠀠
英文版本則是 Designed a compound engineering workflow that automatically extracts patterns from AI agent conversations into a reusable skill library, reducing repetitive cod
